20 Ways to Work With JavaScript Arrays (And Which Ones Mutate the Original)
This post came out of watching a video called
20 ways to work with JS arrays
(in Mandarin). There's one chart in it that's honestly pure gold.
So I figured I'd just write a post and note down all 20 ways to work with arrays.

takes the data you want to add inside the parens.)

any = ['A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'E'];
any.shift(); // grabs the first item, A
any.unshift("A"); // puts A back at the front
any

And now you can see the array is back to the original.
If you want to pull out the
// pulls out the last item, E
And to put something back as the last item? Use

any = ['A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'E'];
any.pop(); // pulls out the last item, E
any.push("E"); // puts E back at the end
any
// puts E back at the end
is a bit special — it can do two different things.
Get (or delete) items at a specific position
If you want to delete items at a specific index or position, use
The first is the starting index
The second is how many items to delete (or pull out). If you leave it off, it just takes (deletes) everything from the start index to the end.

any = ['A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'E'];
any.splice(2); // start from index 2 — you'll see it returns C, D, E
// start from index 2 — you'll see it returns C, D, E
, only A and B are left.
But what if you only want to pull out (or delete) one item? Just pass the second argument.

any = ['A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'E'];
any.splice(2,1);  // start at index 2 and take just one item, which is C
// start at index 2 and take just one item, which is C
Now if you check the original
again, you'll see C is gone.
is that second argument — leave it off and it takes/deletes everything from the start index onward.)

Or keep only the first 3 items:
By default, turning an array into a string joins everything with commas.
But if you want to use something other than a comma — a slash, for example — use

any = ['A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'E']; 
any.join('/'); // you can also pass an empty string
// you can also pass an empty string
basically feels like accumulating. The most common use is adding up all the numbers in an array, and it kind of works like a loop.

Heads up: if you pass strings, you just get string concatenation instead.
accumulates from the first item to the last, there's also a method that goes the other way — from the last item back to the first.
With numbers you usually can't tell the difference.
But with strings, the difference is obvious.
is mainly for getting the index position of an item in the array.

any = ['A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'E']; 
any.indexOf('B');  // indexOf starts at 0, so A is 0 and B is 1
// indexOf starts at 0, so A is 0 and B is 1
If it can't find the item, it returns -1.
indexes start at 0, so 0 already means A. If "not found" returned 0, it'd clash with A — so anything not found returns -1 instead.)
returns as soon as it finds a match
scans the whole array and returns the index of the
So for example, say there are three A's:

any = ['A', 'B', 'A', 'D', 'A']; 
any.indexOf('A');  // indexOf returns the first A it finds
// indexOf returns the first A it finds

any = ['A', 'B', 'A', 'D', 'A']; 
any.lastIndexOf('A');  // lastIndexOf finds the last one, so the last A is at index 4
// lastIndexOf finds the last one, so the last A is at index 4
walks the entire array before returning, while
returns the moment it finds something and doesn't bother scanning the rest.)

This post is based on a video called "20 ways to work with JS arrays" — the chart in it is honestly pure gold. So I figured I'd write it all down: 20 array methods, split into the ones that might mutate your original array and the ones that leave it alone. Lots of small hands-on examples you can run in the console.
